week12.Rmd

Kyrene

2026-04-20

Script Settings and Resources

set.seed(1234) # set seed for reproducibility
setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
library(tidyverse)

Data Import and Cleaning

# I used this to download at least the last year of posts from r/IOPsychology.
# url <- "https://www.reddit.com/r/IOPsychology/new.json?limit=100"
# after <- NULL
# every_post <- list()
# cutoff_time <- Sys.time() - as.difftime(365, units = "days")
# done <- FALSE
# 
# repeat { # I use repeat() to create an infinite loop so it stops when I stop it with break. Keep requesitng pages and save each page until there are no more pages or reach to the point that it older than one year 
#   full_url <- paste0(url, if (!is.null(after)) paste0("&after=", after) else "")
#   res <- fromJSON(full_url)
#   posts <- res$data$children$data %>%
#     as_tibble()
#   
#   every_post[[length(every_post) + 1]] <- posts
#   after <- res$data$after
#   
#   oldest_post <- min(as.POSIXct(posts$created_utc, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC"))
#   if (is.null(after) || oldest_post <= cutoff_time) {
#     break
#   }
#   # this reduces the chance of rate limits
#   Sys.sleep(1)
# }
# 
# reddit_posts <- bind_rows(every_post)
# saveRDS(reddit_posts, "../data/reddit_posts.rds")
# 
# in the tibble, I only keep varibales that are required, convert unix time to normal time, keep posts from last year, and make sure it only has only upvotes and title
# week12_tbl <- reddit_posts %>%
#   select(title, upvotes = ups, created_utc) %>%
#   mutate(created_utc = as.POSIXct(created_utc, origin = "1970-01-01", tz = "UTC")) %>%
#   filter(created_utc > cutoff_time) %>%
#   select(upvotes, title)
# write_csv(week12_tbl, "../data/week12_tbl.csv")

# add code to import those files instead of downloading again

reddit_posts <- readRDS("../data/reddit_posts.rds")
week12_tbl <- read_csv("../data/week12_tbl.csv")
## Rows: 417 Columns: 2
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (1): title
## dbl (1): upvotes
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
# Create corpus from post titles, so later the original and preproccessed version can be compared.
io_corpus_original <- VCorpus(VectorSource(week12_tbl$title))

# Preprocessing using tm and qdap by building the transformer that removes references (IO). These terms appear frequently but do not distinguish the posts meaningfully. I think this is not too aggresive. regex might be more apprioproate here?
remove_io_terms <- content_transformer(function(x) {
  x %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bi\\s*/\\s*o\\s+psychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bi\\s*/\\s*o\\s+psych\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bi\\s*-\\s*o\\s+psychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bi\\s*-\\s*o\\s+psych\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bio\\s+psychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bio\\s+psych\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\biopsychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bindustrial\\s+and\\s+organizational\\s+psychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bindustrial\\s+organizational\\s+psychology\\b", " ") %>%
    str_replace_all("\\bindustrial[- ]organizational\\s+psychology\\b", " ")
})

# I then created the preproccessed corpus. First, I expand abbreviations and contractions. Then, lowercase text so they are not case-sensitive. 
# Then, I use the cutome transformer to remove IO terms. Then, I remove numbers and punctuation becuase they are not that meaningful for post titles.
# Then, I lemmatize after punctuation & number removal, so words are simplified. I then remove stopwords after lemmatization. finally, I strip the extra whitespace at the end
io_corpus <- io_corpus_original %>%
  tm_map(content_transformer(replace_abbreviation)) %>%
  tm_map(content_transformer(replace_contraction)) %>%
  tm_map(content_transformer(str_to_lower)) %>%
  tm_map(remove_io_terms) %>%
  tm_map(removeNumbers) %>%
  tm_map(removePunctuation) %>%
  tm_map(content_transformer(lemmatize_strings)) %>%
  tm_map(removeWords, stopwords("en")) %>%
  tm_map(stripWhitespace)
## Registered S3 methods overwritten by 'textclean':
##   method           from
##   print.check_text qdap
##   print.sub_holder qdap
# Function to compare the same randomly selected row from original corpus and the processed corpus.
# I repeatedly check whether preprocessing is doing its work cleanly
compare_them <- function(corpus_1, corpus_2) {
   i <- sample(1:length(corpus_1), 1)
  print(corpus_1[[i]]$content)
  print(corpus_2[[i]]$content)
}
compare_them(io_corpus_original, io_corpus)
## [1] "Searching Remote Research Opportunity to Strengthen PhD Application (IO Psych &amp; OB)"
## [1] "search remote research opportunity strengthen phd application amp ob"
# Create a document id so each title stays linked to its row
io_text_tbl <- tibble(
  document = 1:length(io_corpus),
  text = sapply(io_corpus, as.character)
)

# Unigrams are used to capture individual high-frequency concepts.
io_unigram_tbl <- io_text_tbl %>%
  unnest_tokens(term, text, token = "words") %>%
  count(document, term)

# Bigrams are used ton capture multiword concepts
io_bigram_tbl <- io_text_tbl %>%
  unnest_tokens(term, text, token = "ngrams", n = 2) %>%
  count(document, term)

# Combine into one DTM called io_dtm and filter out any blank or missing terms that might result from preprocessing.
io_dtm <- bind_rows(io_unigram_tbl, io_bigram_tbl) %>%
  filter(!is.na(term), term != "") %>%
  group_by(document, term) %>%
  summarise(n = sum(n), .groups = "drop") %>%
  cast_dtm(document, term, n)

# Sparsity trimming
io_slim_dtm <- removeSparseTerms(io_dtm, .995)

# Check N/k ratio and adjust sparsity threshold to 0.995 to make it into the range 2:1 to 3:1
n_docs <- nrow(io_slim_dtm)
k_terms <- ncol(io_slim_dtm)
n_docs / k_terms
## [1] 2.303867
#  2.303867

Visualization

# I summrize total term frequencies across the full DTM and then produce a workdcloud. This can help me see what dominates discussion (only the words)
wordCounts <- slam::col_sums(io_dtm)
wordNames <- colnames(io_dtm)
wordcloud::wordcloud(wordNames, wordCounts, max.words = 50)

Analysis

# I convert the dtm and then search for all possible topics. I then save the topic result plot into figs subfoolder, adjust the margins, draw the plot and close the graph so the file is actually written.
dfm2stm <- readCorpus(io_slim_dtm, type="slam")
kresult <- searchK(
  dfm2stm$documents,
  dfm2stm$vocab,
  K = seq(2, 20, by = 2)
)
## Warning in stm(documents = heldout$documents, vocab = heldout$vocab, K = k, :
## K=2 is equivalent to a unidimensional scaling model which you may prefer.

## Model Converged
png("../figs/kresult_plot.png", width = 1200, height = 900, res = 150)
par(mar = c(4, 4, 1, 1))
plot(kresult)
dev.off()
## quartz_off_screen 
##                 2
# I fit the final topic model with 6 topics.
# I choose 6 not 7 becuase 6 is better on semantic coherence and residuals 
# I then extract theta, the document-topic probability matrix and assigne it to topic_probs so each row is one document and each column is one topic
# For each document, I can identify topics with their highest probability and extract the probability of that most likely topics
topic_model <- stm(dfm2stm$documents, 
                   dfm2stm$vocab, 
                   6)
## Beginning Spectral Initialization 
##   Calculating the gram matrix...
##   Finding anchor words...
##      ......
##   Recovering initialization...
##      .
## Initialization complete.

## Completed M-Step. 
## Model Converged
topic_probs <- topic_model$theta
assign_topic <- apply(topic_probs, 1, which.max)
assign_prob <- apply(topic_probs, 1, max)
keep_rows <- rowSums(as.matrix(io_slim_dtm)) > 0

# inspect the fitted topics using top words, representative titles, topic prevalence, and topic correlations
labelTopics(topic_model, n = 10)
## Topic 1 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: master, work, want, assessment, datum, io master, hr, submission, step, community 
##       FREX: master, work, want, assessment, datum, io master, submission, step, community, will 
##       Lift: work, want, submission, manager, assessment, psychological, master, datum, next, next step 
##       Score: recommendation, master, work, io master, next, next step, step, want, assessment, will 
## Topic 2 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: think, read, biweekly, biweekly r, discussion, discussion read, r, r discussion, read think, good 
##       FREX: think, read, biweekly, biweekly r, discussion, discussion read, r, r discussion, read think, question 
##       Lift: biweekly, biweekly r, discussion, discussion read, r, r discussion, read think, online, question, read 
##       Score: biweekly, biweekly r, discussion, discussion read, r, r discussion, read think, read, think, decision 
## Topic 3 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: job, phd, look, siop, psychologist, ai, program, psych, s, find 
##       FREX: job, ai, program, psych, find, apply, pursue, year, look, major 
##       Lift: apply, pursue, year, ai, bachelor, job, major, real, tool, program 
##       Score: job, salary, phd, look, program, siop, psychologist, s, psych, find 
## Topic 4 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: career, need, get, io psychologist, field, new, career advice, student, survey, degree 
##       FREX: career, io psychologist, field, career advice, survey, seek, path, someone, grad, need 
##       Lift: path, someone, grad, career, start, mba, seek, still, career path, survey 
##       Score: career, just, need, career advice, get, new, seek, path, student, career path 
## Topic 5 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: io, advice, help, market, graduate, internship, job market, school, feel, impact 
##       FREX: help, internship, job market, school, io, india, build, market, feel, advice 
##       Lift: help, india, school, build, internship, free, entry, entry level, level, job market 
##       Score: io, advice, book, help, market, job market, graduate, internship, school, io job 
## Topic 6 Top Words:
##       Highest Prob: psychology, people, can, organizational, amp, research, much, analytics, experience, worth 
##       FREX: psychology, people, organizational, amp, much, analytics, experience, people analytics, role, search 
##       Lift: people, psychology, organizational, state, interview, organizational psychology, application, consult, amp, go 
##       Score: score, psychology, people, organizational, analytics, amp, can, much, people analytics, experience
findThoughts(topic_model, texts = week12_tbl$title[keep_rows], n = 3)
## 
##  Topic 1: 
##       Skills assessment for coding will become legal risks
##      Next Steps
##      AMA - HR Director, Hiring Manager, ATS Owner 
##  Topic 2: 
##       Bi-Weekly /r/IOpsychology Discussion - What have you been reading, and what do you think of it?
##      Bi-Weekly /r/IOpsychology Discussion - What have you been reading, and what do you think of it?
##      Bi-Weekly /r/IOpsychology Discussion - What have you been reading, and what do you think of it? 
##  Topic 3: 
##       Looking for Current/Prospective student's to apply to our PhD Program. Binghamton University PhD in Management
##      Business Psych major or do I stay a Psych major?
##      In final year of Masters program, how far in advance to apply for jobs? 
##  Topic 4: 
##       🎓 Current I-O Psychology Grad Student Seeking Real-World Insight — Is This the Right Career Path?
##      I/O Psychology Career Paths
##      Seeking career advice 
##  Topic 5: 
##       Feeling hopeless trying to land an IO job in the UK. Need advice
##      Entry level jobs for non graduate degree holders in IO Psych?
##      The Impact of Social and Informational Biases on Job-Search Decision-Making 
##  Topic 6: 
##       Searching Remote Research Opportunity to Strengthen PhD Application (IO Psych &amp; OB)
##      What do you believe is the most helpful textbook or resource in learning organizational psychology?
##      Preparing for a Sr. People Analytics Consultant interview!
plot(topic_model, type = "summary", n = 6)

topic_correlation <- topicCorr(topic_model)
plot(topic_correlation)

# based on top words, I created labels and assign them into topic labels
topic_labels <- c(
  "1" = "Programs' Value & Degree values",
  "2" = "Research Opportunities & Helps",
  "3" = "People Analytics Careers",
  "4" = "Career Advice For Graduate",
  "5" = "Job Market",
  "6" = "Biweekly Discussion"
)

# Then, I created the tibble topics_tbl that has doc_id, original, topic, topic_label, probability, upvotes
topics_tbl <- tibble(
  doc_id = which(keep_rows),
  original = week12_tbl$title[keep_rows],
  topic = assign_topic,
  topic_label = topic_labels[as.character(assign_topic)],
  probability = assign_prob,
  upvotes = week12_tbl$upvotes[keep_rows]
)

Explain in detail the specific reasoning process you used to determine the final number of topics

I use searchK() to compare models across a range of topic numbers from K = 2 to K = 20 by 2 I start at 2 because K = 1 cannot meaningfully separate the posts into different themes I then compare heldout likelihood, semantic coherence (semcoh), exclusivity (exclus), and residual-related measures Based on these diagnostics, K = 6 gives the best overall balance between model fit and interpretability. It performsn better on semantic coherence and residuals and still keeps the topics reasonably distinct and easy to interpret I therefore choose 6 because it separates the major themes well without making the topics too broad or too narrow

Explain in detail the specific reasoning process for the topic labels you selected

I select the topic labels by looking at the top words in each topic, so I can understand what the main content in each topic is I then check whether those top words match the actual post titles that are assigned to that topic I use the top words and the titles together to figure out the main idea of each topic and then give each topic a short label to represent the idea I also make the labels easy to understand, concise, and close to what the posts in each topic are about (2 to 5 words)

Mahcine Learning

## 
## Call:
## summary.resamples(object = resamples)
## 
## Models: Tokens only, Tokens + topics 
## Number of resamples: 10 
## 
## MAE 
##                     Min.  1st Qu.    Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. NA's
## Tokens only     6.721313 9.617101  9.930054 12.37437 13.87447 24.15172    0
## Tokens + topics 7.269160 9.056513 10.236193 13.14188 15.85343 27.90679    0
## 
## RMSE 
##                     Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. NA's
## Tokens only     8.418815 11.36277 13.83131 19.87647 27.20132 44.17333    0
## Tokens + topics 8.267313 11.23584 13.44236 20.13232 28.89760 44.99609    0
## 
## Rsquared 
##                        Min.    1st Qu.     Median       Mean    3rd Qu.
## Tokens only     0.019021853 0.03989477 0.04991546 0.08118517 0.09116667
## Tokens + topics 0.002975994 0.01589439 0.08667640 0.11247109 0.19836191
##                      Max. NA's
## Tokens only     0.2580482    0
## Tokens + topics 0.3376984    0

Comment:

adding topics improves cv results but does not improve out-of-sample performance prediction. Both models perform poorly but the tokens plus topics model is slightly worse in the holdout set. CV improvement does not mean it can be generalized to new data.
